title: 2025 11 27 FEC
displayTitle: 2025-11-27 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Reserve Bank of New Zealand Monetary Policy Statement November 2025
topic: Reserve Bank of New Zealand Monetary Policy Statement November 2025
description: Thursday 27 November 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Reserve Bank of New Zealand Monetary Policy Statement November 2025 Reserve Bank of New Zealand Karen Silk, Assistant Governor, Money Paul Conway, Chief Economist Christian Hawkesby, Acting Governor
descriptionSummary: Reserve Bank of New Zealand Monetary Policy Statement November 2025. Reserve Bank of New Zealand. Karen Silk, Assistant Governor, Money

chunks.text: Qui chair. Can you hear us? Uh, we can hear you, Mr. Hawkes be, we can't quite see you at this stage, but I'm there you are. Yeah. Okay. So, um, uh, welcome. We are, we are in your hands. Uh, we'd just like to introduce Mr. Hawkes, be here. Uh, and also joined by assistant governor, uh, Karen Silk, and of course, chief economist Paul Conway, uh, for this, the monetary policy, um, assessment, uh, November, uh, by the FEC committee, the day following.

So, welcome. Thank you, chair. Uh, Koto. Koto. Uh, I'll keep, uh, opening comments very brief 'cause you've, uh, had the document, uh, and, and sure have had a look. We see the outlook for 2026 as one where, uh, CPI inflation will be, uh, within the band, uh, target range and falling towards, uh, 2% by mid next year.

And one where the economy is expanding, uh, building on the momentum that we are already seeing through the second half of this year. So, for the first time in quite a while, we're in a position where we have, uh, a central projection where risks are balanced both to the upside and to the downside.

And we've published a, a projection for the official cash rate, which is around the level we are now, uh, through the course of next year. So after reducing rates by over 300 basis points in recently, short time, uh, we think we're in a position where we've got the best chance to, uh, sit back and, and monetary policy can be in the background in 2026.

I'll leave it there chair. And we're very, uh, happy to take questions. If you direct them to me, I will bring, uh, the team in as much as possible. Okay. Thank you. Mr. Hawkes. Be, we're going to go to the opposition, uh, lead spokesperson, the Honorable Barbara Edmonds Kia, everyone. Um, thank you. Um, I think in appear before Christian, is that right?
Oh, sorry, Barbara, you just cut out there.

You might, you might be on a bad line. Here we go. Have you freed up now? Can you hear me now? Yeah, If, if you can just repeat that. Sorry, Barbara. Yeah, so I was just asking, you know, is this, um, sort of your commentary sort of points to that where the, at the bottom of the easing cycle and, and there are some indicators for both upside and downside risk.

How does that reconcile with, um, obviously more recent commentary since your announcement yesterday, which are who are worried that your statement is a little bit more hawkish and that it may put upward pressure on morga on the OCR? Yeah, so we were put out a central projection that we think is, uh, consistent with the economic data that, that we are seeing.

We we're conscious that there had been some expectation that we might wanna keep the door more open to further cuts. Uh, but we really have to, you know, play what's in front of us. And, and we think, uh, we are in a position where we can, uh, put out a an OCR projection, which is broadly unchanged because that is consistent with our mandate in the sense of we've got confidence that it will see, uh, the economy expanding and inflation coming back to the midpoint of the target.

It's one that we, we think we're in a position where, uh, we can sort of watch, worry and wait, see how things, uh, progress over the course of next year. As always, there are upside and downside risks around that. Uh, the difference this time around is that we've already got the official cash rate, you know, much lower than it was at the beginning of the year, and that gives us that greater flexibility.

Okay. And, and in, and in relation to that flexibility, I noted that one of your NPC members dissented, uh, uh, was it five to one? I think the decision was, um, just wanting to understand how that, um, that particular dissenter, what, not necessarily what they were saying, but just how were they getting to that decision point where they thought that actually they were going to dissent?

And I think it's a good thing that there isn't group think on the NPC 'cause this is, I think, the second time that this has happened. Yeah, Uh, that's right. So we, we've actually, there's been more voting in, in recent times. We've voted, uh, three times in the last six, uh, meetings while I've been chairing the committee.

Uh, there, there is a threshold to vote, which is, you know, the, the committee member feeling uncomfortable about being part of a consensus and, and taking a different decision. Uh, this time around, uh, the committee discussed two options. Uh, one was to leave the official cash rate unchanged. Uh, the other was to lower it by 25 basis points.

Uh, that that member who, uh, dissented was, uh, in the camp of leaving the official cash rate unchanged at this meeting, but leaving the door open, uh, to, to lower it, uh, in the future if required. So it was in part, uh, part around timing, uh, in part around different members will often have a different emphasis on upside and downside risks.

So this particular member had a little bit more emphasis on the upside risks, uh, to inflation and the economy, uh, and a, a a different view around timing. I mean, because I mean, I, I can understand how they sort of, um, got to that level given that sort of inflation has increased, not as much as previously, but it has been increasing over the last few quarters.

My, my, um, next question. Sorry, Mr. Chair, if that's all right. That's alright. Um, there's been a little bit of co um, discussion around the transparency of MPC decisions and whether we should have MPC committee members brief select committees and, you know, around transparency. Do you have any particular views in relation to that? Yeah, I, I think we've been on a journey when I, when I look back at my seven years on the committee, the, the, the first charter for the, we have a remit, which is around what we're trying to achieve

and what our objectives are. And then we have a charter, which is about really how we operate as a committee. Um, seven years ago, the charter was very restrictive around, uh, what committee members could say and when they could say, and, uh, when they could say things. Through time, it's become more permissive.

Uh, and we've had the likes of Prassana guy, one of our external members, uh, talking more frequently, uh, externally, I think, I think we're on a path to become more encouraging, uh, to get more members out there talking, uh, within the committee. We just don't really think of ourselves of it as internals and, and externals.

We don't really see that distinction. And it would be good to get that across publicly as well by more committee members, uh, talking. What I would, uh, you know, provide some caution around is going down a path where, uh, we have a lot of committee members, uh, effectively saying which way they're gonna vote before we've even got together and discussed what's the right thing to do.

And that's the position, uh, that, that I see overseas. And, and it's one where you don't actually have a monetary com policy committee listening to each other, working together as a team, really trying to do the right thing by the country, uh, collectively. And you have more a bunch of individuals, uh, you know, doing their own thing.

So I think if we become more open, more transparent, but just be really careful that we don't lose, uh, the really good things about the process that we've, uh, evolved over the last seven years. Thank you. And last, last question, if that's right, Mr. Chief, and then just, uh, separate topic.

Um, obviously the economic indicators show some improvement in different sectors, but faster than other sectors. I mean, we've heard the word green shoots, I think for the last 18 months. Mm-hmm. And for a lot of people, they don't feel the green shoots in their wallets. And right now it's still really tough for a lot of people.

Yeah. So when you talk about spare capacity in the economy, can you break that down for the average person on the street, what that means, uh, in just normal terms, like what is their capacity and how does that impact on people? 'cause they're wanting to see relief, but they've not, some people in the urban areas not feeling it faster than those perhaps in the rural sector.

Yeah. Uh, so it's an acknowledgement that at times have been tough. It has been a tough period. Uh, it, it, that has been a weak labor market. It's been difficult to, uh, find work, uh, unemployment has risen. Uh, we are not using, uh, the, the people and resources that, that we can, uh, fully, uh, that's what, um, spare capacity means to us.

And, and what it, uh, points to from a, from a monetary policy point of view is, you know, a, a requirement for us to kickstart things, get interest rates a lower, get some activity going. What we see is that when, when demand picks up, uh, businesses, invest, businesses employ all of those things to, um, you know, make full use of the resources that we have.

I'll, I'll get, uh, Paul to talk a little bit about the special topic, uh, that we've got in there on the labor market, which I think is really valuable because it kind of gets under the bonnet, uh, of, of what spare capacity means, uh, for the labor market and for the people of New Zealand. Thanks, uh, governor.

So, but like, just building on that, from a, from a business perspective, spare capacity, you know, essentially means a lack of orders. They could be cranking a little bit harder if, if not, if, if people wanted to buy their product, uh, in the labor market for people. Obviously unemployment, uh, is up, you know, that's, uh, there's a whole bunch of indicators in the labor market around spare capacity.

So, you know, people, um, are more likely to be unemployed. Uh, it's more difficult for unemployed people to get back in the labor market currently. So the job hiring rate, uh, is quite low. Um, you know, there's not that many vacancies, uh, available per unemployed person.

Uh, it's again, you know, it's just a, a period where it feels like our resources could be employed more fully. Um, we do, we do think we're at, at the trough or, you know, passing through the trough of, of that output gap, that spare capacity has sort of been at, at its maximum, um, touch wood. Uh, and that that output gap, uh, is closing less spare capacity in the economy going forward.

Okay. Thank you. I've got six hands up here. I'm gonna go to Chloe Swarbrick Morning. Um, on page 34, you say, and I quote, compared to history, it is hard for unemployed people to transition back to employment end quote. Yeah. I guess could you unpack for us how this sits historically? Uh, and why?

Yep. Um, So where are we? I'm just looking at those, uh, graphs. So yeah, the job finding rate. I think, uh, Chloe, you're talking about S 30.5. Yeah. Well, I'm talking about your quote from page 34 where you talk about it being hard for unemployed people to transition back to employment compared to history.

And I'm just wondering if you could discern a few of the variables as to why. Yeah. So this, you know, we described this, or we talked about it yesterday in the press conference, uh, as a, a low hire, low fire labor market in New Zealand. Um, so businesses are, you know, sort of holding onto labor, given that not so long ago we went through a period of, uh, very, a very tight, uh, labor market in terms of employees, in terms of unemployed people.

Uh, the chances of finding a job of transitioning from being unemployed to having a job is, uh, is, is very low, uh, at the moment. So unemployment has peaked, or hopefully at around 5.3%, we think that's gonna sort of hang in there over the December quarter and into early next year before improving as kind of a lagging, uh, indicator.

Um, and if, which is lower than it was in the global financial crisis, um, for example, um, you know, which yeah. But the, the probability of finding a job is, is, is low for people, uh, at the moment. In fact, it's a bit lower, uh, if you look at that graph than it was, um, during that 2008, uh, period.

And I guess further to that point then, I mean, how would you determine the efficacy of monetary policy in particularly the OCR on fixing something like that? Um, like I, I think, you know, the, the OCR, the job of the Central bank, it's all about controlling the business cycle, uh, and not, uh, sort of eliminating the business cycle.

It's about sort of chopping the peaks and the troughs, uh, off with the objective of keeping inflation at that 2% level. So, you know, we absolutely, with changes in the OCR, we do put a cyclical impulse through the economy, uh, including the labor market. Mm-hmm. Um, as opposed to more structural settings.

So there's countries that just have a lower rate of unemployment, for example, in general across the cycle compared to New Zealand or countries where it's just easier for people to find a job because those structural settings, uh, in the labor market, um, you know, work, work well. And could you just name, um, what it is that you're talking about there, Paul, when you're talking about those structural settings, just to use that language so that regular people at home can follow?

Yeah, I think like some of the Nordic countries, they invest in, uh, act active, what we call active labor market policies, where You're talking about government fiscal policy there and the limitations of monetary. I'm not, I'm not talking about fiscal spending. I'm, I'm talking about the sort of support services that are, are wrapped around someone is who is unemployed, and how much effort goes into transitioning them back into the workforce education skills, All of those micro bottom up factors.

And what I would acknowledge is that we, if you look back over ly over the course of the last couple of years, we have been, uh, slowing the economy, uh, because inflation was too high. Uh, and we've been, you know, high interest rates have been part of that process. We now are coming through the other side.

Uh, these, and the labor market indicators will be one of the last ones to, to turn. We've got the special topic that that shows that there's much more going on that just the headline unemployment rate. There are a number of labor market indicators that are starting to pick up. Um, so vacancies, hours worked, hours paid, all of these things are starting to turn.

And so as, as we go through next year, that's something that we'll be looking, looking to the influence of monetary policy on the labor market's going to be positive rather than negative, which is a nice place to be. And, uh, and then separate to that, there are all these structural issues that are outside of our control that, that set, you know, those deeper parameters.

If I may further to that point, Mr. Chair. Yeah, yeah. Final question, then we've gotta move on. Yeah. Okay. How do you assess then the risk that the bank settings are potentially stimulating asset markets more than real economic activity or un uh, or employment? Uh, it's just something that we constantly monitor.

The, the transmission mechanism of monetary policy. We know it works through many different channels. And, and so we, so we monitor that, uh, if anything the, in this latest, um, easing, we've seen less activity through that wealth channel. Uh, you know, house prices haven't increased, uh, by nearly as much as they have in previous, uh, cycles.

And if anything, they've been being flat, it's been a missing element. And that the other, the other channels are doing the work, the cashflow channel, the exchange rate channel is one that people haven't been talking about a lot, but, which is very important in the way that our interest rates have been lowered relative to other countries. That's helped the exchange rate, uh, be lower and, and support the economy, uh, through that channel.

Yeah. So it's a case of constantly monitoring them. Are they in totality doing enough to achieve our, uh, inflation, uh, mandate That's from a, a rate perspective, but, um, the reserve bank can't direct the banks where to, um, lend money that that's a decision they make as a business.

That's a commercial decision. Um, but what I would say is that credit supply is not the issue in the market today. There is plenty of supply there. They have the capacity, they have the capital, they have the liquidity to be able to do it. It's credit demand, particularly in the business sector.

So what we've seen is with that increased uncertainty, particularly that we saw through the mid part of this year, um, with that uncertainty, business investment really stalled. And what they're looking for businesses themselves before they start, uh, seeking more credit and, uh, investing more in their businesses is for that demand to pick up.

So they want to see more consumption, uh, you know, people spending in order. And when that picks up, that's when you get that transfer through. So the first indicators you are looking for is, are we seeing people spending more? And that will, that will start to drive activity when activity comes up, then you start to see those, those labor indicators that Christian was talking about, um, start to to change as well.

And we are starting to see some of that. And that's when you would expect to see that broaden through 2026. Right. Okay. Thank you. Um, Karen, we're gonna go to Dr. Deborah Russell. Uh, thank you. Excuse me, Mr. Chair. Um, I want to, um, just a little bit more on the under capacity.

Um, uh, I think the phrase you used, um, a couple, a few questions. There's a phrase that was used, low hire, low fire, which is, um, uh, what's happening in, in employment at the moment. Um, but do you have a sense of to what extent as well, employees are just sitting tight and choosing not to move because, or choosing not to risk a job they have already instead of maybe going somewhere else?

Yeah, absolutely. That's exactly what is happening. If you look at, there's a, uh, figure 4.6, uh, on page 37, we've got a, a graph in there of Jo job transitions, so we can see when people change employers. Uh, and you can see that was, that was very high, uh, coming out of the COVID pandemic.

Uh, and then into, into the sort of business cycle contraction that we've been living through, um, the last 18 months or so. Uh, it's very low. So, so definitely there's a sense of people staying put, uh, in their job be because of uncertainty. It's kind of a precautionary, uh, you know, rational precautionary behavior. Yep. Thank you.

Um, that seemed plausible to me as well. I just want to check out, I had the right understanding there. You said that, um, unemployment is predicted to continue at about 5.3% over December and into early next year. And, um, so that's, um, means people just don't have money in their back pockets or they're scared they're gonna not have a job or something like that.

But of course, that has a flow on impact into the retail sector. Do you have a sense of how many firms are kind of hoping for a good Christmas, um, or how many firms are just hanging in there? Um, Yeah, All of them hoping, but it's kind got me worried, um, in that sector. And yeah, I just wanna give you some assurance.

The we are not waiting for a recovery. A a recovery is happening right at the moment. So, uh, Q2, yes, there was a contraction in Q2. Uh, there's a whole lot of, uh, quirks and statistical reasons why we think that contraction wasn't nearly as bad as the headline.

Uh, negative 0.9. We do think there was a contraction, not nearly that bad. Uh, all our indicators are telling us now that, uh, the economy expanded through Q3, which is in the rear vision mirror. You know, we're in Q4 now, uh, and over the second half of the year is running at an annualized rate, not far of short 3%.

So we're, we're getting that, uh, up uptick. We're getting that momentum. We're not seeing the official numbers, uh, yet. What we're also aware of just coming back to the labor market, those labor market, uh, indicators, as Karen explained, uh, will tend to be the last ones you see moving.

And it's, it's typically around a six month type lag that you see between the economic activity expanding and, uh, those coming through on the labor market. So this, this is a normal, uh, this is a normal dynamic that, that you would expect to see. And we've got confidence around, uh, where things will be as we get into next year.

And, and thank you. Um, in, in terms of that, um, I was intrigued, um, because of course you have the, the mandate of keeping inflation within the band, and if we, there's, there's gonna be pressure on that if the economy's picking up. And I did notice that the last, um, uh, inflation figure was officially 3.0, that was by the grace of the rounding gods.

Um, there was, there was one point in it on the CPI measure the CPI being one point different. The inflation measure would've been 3.1%. So, um, uh, how did that play into the c uh, the OCR decision that you made? Yeah, So, no, that, that's a wonderful question. 'cause I, I think it really highlights the two things that as a committee we've been balancing all year.

Uh, and, and, and one of them is this, you know, issue about spare capacity and, and a bit of a worry that consumers, uh, excessively consumers and households are in a bit of a funk and they're excessively cautious on the one side. Uh, the other side has been, uh, this sort of echo of high inflation and that, and the memory of high inflation and, and the influence that may have on price and, uh, price setting behavior.

Uh, and so those are the two things that we've been balancing. Uh, one of the changes in this, I think this latest decision really marks is, is a sense that we have moved through that danger zone in, in a sense that, uh, yes, headline inflation has blipped up a bit close to 3%, but it hasn't followed through into price setting behavior, uh, inflation expectations, core inflation measures are falling and, and have been falling.

Uh, and we've got a high, reasonably high degree of confidence that, uh, those next few quarters are gonna be coming down from 3%. So it's, it's really a story that given the pressure on inflation through that channel is abating it, it means that we, we actually need to create some inflation, uh, um, through stronger economic activity such that we, you know, balance those two and hit, hit the 2%.

Okay. So some engineering in there, That's a factor. Absolutely. Is one last question if I may, Mr. Chair, just one? Yeah, We've got six. We've still got six hands up. We keep multiplying, but Promise it's just one. Um, um, uh, so, um, uh, a really difficult balancing task. Um, I noticed that, um, the big banks have immediately dropped their floating rates, but their fixed rates haven't changed yet.

And I think the two year swap rate hasn't particularly, I don't follow it exactly. Can you comment on what's going on there? What you think might be behind their thinking? 'cause of course. Um, yeah, Yeah. Um, so, so the, uh, OCR, so the official cash rate is really has, um, quite strong influence on that short term part of the interest rate curve.

And the further you go out, it's other factors that start to influence it as well. So our greatest impact is in that short, in that short end. Um, so the, the, the roughly 10 basis point movement that we've seen in the swap curve, uh, in the two year that we've seen since the announcement yesterday is not a surprise, uh, to us at all.

Um, actually a, a reasonable chunk of that is, was an offshore overnight, and that's just the offshore investors. Um, they'll be forming a decision. So when you are getting near, near or at that turning point in the cycle, the role that markets start playing is they start looking at, well, what's the next move going to be?

And if they start thinking it's an upward move, then they'll start trying to position themselves for that. So there's a little bit of that going on at this point in time. Um, likewise, uh, you know, we may now start to see more movement in the, um, from consumers, households moving their mortgage rates out a little bit further as well.

And that may also provide a little bit of upward pressure on that wholesale rate because banks will try and, um, hedge the risk that they take on as a consequence of that. Um, so that's on that side of it. On the other side of it, it's gonna take a little, you know, it will still take a little while for this credit growth to start to, and demand to start to pick up.

So, um, in that repricing phase where we still have, you know, we think something like 40% home loans over the kind of next nine months or so, um, need to reprice, there'll be competition for that. So, so, um, as we, you know, as we say, like there's, there's, um, whilst mar margins might be back to kind of the average historic level, it's still a healthy margin.

And so there's capacity there for more competition in terms of the banks competing for those customers who are looking to reset a new rate. And they haven't, they've shown increasing propensity over the years to shift banks in order to get that. Okay, thank you. We're gonna go to, thanks, uh, Deborah, we're gonna go to nationals.

Nancy Lou, Uh, good morning and thank you chair, and thank you for, uh, coming in. Uh, my question is around, um, your opening remarks this morning. You said the economy is expanding, building on the momentum. Now this is the first time that you didn't, well, I didn't quite catch gr green shoots in your opening email, but you actually said momentum.

So I wanted some specific examples that you see, um, and that that will, that you know, that, that, that driven you to give the word of momentum in the economy. Um, and then have a follow up question. Thank you. Yeah. Uh, so it was, it was really a comment about, uh, my opening was talking about 2026, so what's, I know what's likely to happen next year.

Yeah. And that's the, the economy expanding, building on a momentum that we are already seeing in Q3 and Q4, uh, this year. So we, we release a, a Kiwi GDP indicator, which collates all the sort of real time, uh, indicators that we are seeing, and we, um, publish that frequently, that that's, uh, picking a 0.6 for, for Q3.

So again, that's looking in the rear vision mirror, uh, in terms of where things have been, uh, we've got, uh, lots of things coming through the, um, purchasing manager index. The, um, those different employment, uh, indicators that I mentioned, personal consumptions, um, building consents are up.

Uh, so various, um, indicators that, that all come together to build that, that picture of what's not only, um, gonna happen in the future, but what what's actually occurring right now. Thank you. And my follow up question is actually around, um, a comment that was made by, uh, the CEO of port for Auckland who he said, um, the Port have had one of the best month and the best 12 months in the last nine years, and, and the bulk and, and the bulk products that are coming in, including cement, have been the highest that he's seen.

Do you take some of those market, uh, commentary or data to, to add to your analysis? Karen, do you wanna speak to our business? Yeah. Um, so, so our teams, uh, go out on a regular basis and talk to businesses, uh, to understand what they're seeing. Um, there's certainly still a little bit of a mix I would, I would say there, but what, uh, the last round of visits really in, and that, that reflects a little bit of that two speed economy still with the export sector doing, um, better than the domestic, uh, sector, you know, over the,

over the past kind of six months or so. Um, but, uh, what they are starting to see and certainly indicating is, uh, even in the domestic spaces, stabilization. So, um, and they're retaining that positive outlook that they, they are seeing, they expect things to get better.

I think stabilization is, is the key word. Yeah. They see demand stabilizing in the economy very much consistent with that, that green shoots word. Yeah. We, we, we don't use that word, but it's actually not a bad description. Yeah. That's great. Okay. Thank you, Nancy. Thank you so much. We're gonna go to New Zealand first. Uh, Dr. David Wilson, uh, you're on mute, David. That's it.

Thanks chair. Um, really interesting words you used at the beginning. Um, uh, Mr. Sby talking about an expanding economy and reducing interest rates. Um, given what we've been through, uh, which is pretty much the opposite, um, I just want you to make a comment. Um, there was a UBS commentator that said that, um, in his first time in the seat looking at New Zealand from Australia, um, saying that we were perhaps a little conservative and a little bit slow to reduce the a CR and perhaps we, you know, didn't get in there earlier.

So I'd like to get your comment on that, given the success we are now having, well, I would call it success at this stage, looking forward, you know, expanding economy and low interest rates. Yeah. That's interesting. Yeah. Well, thank you. We, we, we do think we're in a good spot, uh, going forward, and we think we're in a spot that, uh, central banks like to be in, uh, and, and other central banks would like to be in.

We all, we all have our turn. Having, being in a good spot or being in a tricky spot. The, um, you know, w the way things have evolved, uh, this year, as I mentioned earlier, there have been risks, uh, e either, either side of the story, uh, and, and the fact that we've been living in that environment where, uh, that echo of high inflation, um, still being around that memory of high inflation still being around did, and getting up close to the top of the band did, did create a risk where, um, inflation expectations could,

and price setting behavior could ratchet up and, and really remove that ability to, to ease policy. Uh, we, we feel like we're coming through the other side of that, which has really marked that shift from, from a quite a cautious, uh, approach through July, uh, through into October where it was a shot in the arm to businesses, uh, and consumers that to, to get out of that excessive caution to one where we are, uh, at the moment where we think there's a really good chance that we can just step into the background, uh,

and let the, you know, let the economy do its thing, get off the front pages, uh, let the, you know, the good news of, uh, New Zealand businesses, um, step to the front. Yep. Look forward to a little bit of an, uh, pressure on interest rates, um, coming into next year.

But second question, just following up, uh, with Dr. Russell's discussion around the bank's reaction to mortgage rates, um, note that the net interest margins have not really shifted that much. And some commentators are telling us that on average we are 1%, uh, above Australia in a similar situation.

So it, it's, it's concerning really that, uh, if you follow on and, and I understand the long term and the two year swap rates and all that kind of thing coming into play, but that said, overall whether things are going well or things are not, uh, starting to look better, we're still stuck with this dead interest we rate margin that we don't seem to be able to get on top of.

Yeah. Yeah. Karen. Yeah. Uh, so near interest margin is, yeah, definitely, um, the right thing in my view to look at, uh, because there are, obviously you need to look at both the, what's happening on a funding cost perspective, um, and we are lending rates are sitting at, and that's where the net interest margin story comes into it.

And I, and you know, New Zealand, um, banks are significantly funded by deposits from within the New Zealand market. And so, uh, where we might see that, uh, you know, at different points in the cycle, you may well see that the gap between retail and wholesale deposit rates, uh, may narrow or expand depending on whether we are in a rising or interest rising interest rate or a falling interest rate, um, scenario.

And that's when at times there's also lags and you might see movement, uh, above and below where that relative to where the average has been. Um, the thing that really changes that is competition, uh, and, you know, that that's, that's the, the biggest thing that, uh, will change the average margin or net interest margin story.
